title: Fossil Fuel Transition: The World Summit and the $9.75 Billion Contradiction
narrative:
**Adversarial Analysis | May 17, 2026**

The Santa Marta conference (April 24-29, 2026) — the first International Conference on Transitioning Away from Fossil Fuels — was hailed by 57 participating countries as a "historic" shift from ambition to action. Colombia's minister declared "fossil fuels bring death." The Netherlands framed transition as an economic and security imperative. The public narrative: the world is finally moving from fossil fuel rhetoric to concrete phase-out roadmaps.

The same week, a Louisiana LNG export terminal took its Final Investment Decision.
telemetry: Commonwealth LNG in Louisiana: $9.75 billion FID, backed by Mubadala Energy, Kimmeridge, CPP Investments
Santa Marta summit: no binding targets, no mandatory timelines, no enforcement mechanisms
Summit explicitly a "non-negotiation forum" — workstreams are voluntary
Countries "invited via email" to report fossil fuel subsidies
Summit's own Action Insights report called for halting all new fossil fuel expansion — ignored
Oil prices surged 3% the same week as the conference closed, driven by Hormuz war risk
ARENA (Australia) hydrogen funding: predominantly grey/blue hydrogen (gas-derived)
1.4C warming above pre-industrial baseline cited by NASA/Copernicus as "early 2026 estimate"
analysis:
Fifty-seven countries called it historic. The same week, $9.75 billion in new LNG infrastructure went contractual.

The Santa Marta output is real in the sense that a conference happened and a declaration was signed. It is not real in the sense of changing anything. No binding targets. No mandatory timelines. No enforcement. Voluntary workstreams where countries report themselves via email. The summit's own analysis called for halting new fossil fuel expansion. Governments present did not act on that recommendation.

Commonwealth LNG's $9.75 billion is not a plan or an aspiration. It is a signed contract for new fossil fuel infrastructure that will operate for decades. That is the capital reality layered on top of the rhetorical urgency.

The language escalated — "fossil fuels bring death" — while the capital flows in the opposite direction. Oil prices surged the same week the conference closed, driven by the Hormuz war. The market does not care about summit declarations when physical supply is actually constrained.

Australia announced hydrogen funding going predominantly to gas-derived hydrogen. The transition language and the transition investment have not converged. They may never converge at the rate currently implied.

The disconnect between what was said at the podium and what was signed in the boardroom is the entire story of this transition so far. They called for an end to new fossil fuel expansion. Then signed off on $9.75 billion for new LNG.
verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT]: They called for an end to new fossil fuel expansion. Then signed off on $9.75 billion for new LNG. The language moved faster than the capital. Santa Marta is evidence that the world can produce impressive rhetoric about ending fossil fuels. Commonwealth LNG is evidence that the world is simultaneously building new fossil fuel infrastructure for decades.
